truthAudit.Summary: Truth audit of Run 2 findings against the visible true vulnerabilities in SuperCalc. Strictly evaluates whether Run 2's claims were present in its output, with exact quotes and no post-hoc rationalization.

Items.Notes: Run 2 explicitly identified the format string vulnerability in log_debug_message with the exact code snippet showing printf(active_format.c_str(), user_input), matching SC-V3-001's anchors (log_format, runtime_log_format, printf).
Run 2 correctly identified the integer overflow in the factorial function with the exact loop and multiplication, matching SC-V3-002's anchors (result *= i; fact).
Run 2 did not identify the Use-After-Free in MemoryPool::cleanup (SC-V3-003). It mentioned memory leaks and race conditions but not the specific UAF tied to stage_deferred_history_flush and cleanup.
Run 2 explicitly identified command injection in config validation with the exact code, matching SC-V3-004's anchors (validator_cmd + " " + config_file; system(command.c_str()); admin validate).
Run 2 identified the stack buffer overflow in input_buffer_ with the exact strcpy call and condition, matching SC-V3-005's anchors (strcpy(input_buffer_; dest[src_len]; src_len > dest_size).
Run 2 did not identify the race condition in ThreadPool counter (SC-V3-006). It found a race condition in MemoryPool::allocate but not the specific volatile long long issue in ThreadSafeCounter.
Run 2 identified the logic bomb in authentication with the exact code, matching SC-V3-007's anchors (login_attempts_ > 5; EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE; authenticated_ = true).
Run 2 did not identify the path traversal in configuration loading (SC-V3-008). It mentioned config_dir_ + filename but not as a path traversal vulnerability.

Run 2 identified the heap overflow in ExpressionParser::parse with the exact strcpy and buffer size, matching SC-V3-009's anchors (buffer_size_ = 256; strcpy(expression_buffer_; schedule_expression_analysis).
Run 2 identified hardcoded admin credentials with the exact code, matching SC-V3-010's anchors (ADMIN_SECRET; SC_ENT_2025_AUTH).
Run 2 did not identify the weak PRNG for session tokens (SC-V3-011). It mentioned session tokens but not the use of std::mt19937 with a limited range.
Run 2 did not identify the thread-unsafe timestamp cache in logging (SC-V3-012). It mentioned cached_time but not the race condition in localtime usage.

Run 2 identified insecure temporary file handling in append_override_audit_event with the exact system() calls, matching SC-V3-013's anchors (TEMP; TMPDIR; supercalc_emergency.log; system(command.c_str())).
Run 2 did not identify persistent authentication state (SC-V3-014). It mentioned authenticated_ and session_token_ but not as a CWE-613 vulnerability.
Run 2 did not identify unhandled exception in variable parsing (SC-V3-015). It mentioned std::stod but not the specific CWE-754 issue in handle_variable_command.
Run 2 did not identify integer underflow in MemoryPool block split (SC-V3-016). It mentioned SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES but not the underflow in fragment.size calculation.
Run 2 did not identify unbounded recursion in parser (SC-V3-017). It mentioned parse_expression and parse_factor but not the CWE-674 issue.
Run 2 did not identify data race in result cache (SC-V3-018). It mentioned result_cache_ and cache_mutex_ but not the specific race condition in evaluate_cached.
Run 2 did not identify TOCTOU in config loading (SC-V3-019). It mentioned std::filesystem::exists and sleep_for but not as a CWE-367 issue.
Run 2 did not identify integer division by zero in command routing (SC-V3-020). It mentioned calculate_route_score but not the division by token_length.

Items.Key: command injection via admin exec command|command injection|cwe-78|critical|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|if (command.substr(0, 4) == "exec") { std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|arbitrary command execution with the privileges of the running process. attackers can execute system commands after gaining admin access.|authenticated admin user executing the 'exec' command with malicious input.|remove the 'exec' command or implement strict input validation and a whitelist of allowed commands. use safer alternatives to system().->command injection via admin exec command|command injection|cwe-78|critical|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|if (command.substr(0, 4) == "exec") { std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|arbitrary command execution with the privileges of the running process. attackers can execute system commands after gaining admin access.|authenticated admin user executing the 'exec' command with malicious input.|remove the 'exec' command or implement strict input validation and a whitelist of allowed commands. use safer alternatives to system().

command injection in emergency override audit logging|command injection|cwe-78|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|622|632|admin::adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|#ifdef _win32 const char* temp_dir = std::getenv("temp"); std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : ".") + "\\supercalc_emergency.log"; std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; #else const char* temp_dir = std::getenv("tmpdir"); std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : "/tmp") + "/supercalc_emergency.log"; std::string command = "sh -c \"echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path + "\""; #endif system(command.c_str());|arbitrary command execution if the temp or tmpdir environment variables contain shell metacharacters. also, the audit_path is not properly escaped.|emergency override authentication triggering the append_override_audit_event function with a manipulated environment.|use secure file operations (e.g., std::ofstream) instead of system() calls. sanitize all paths and avoid shell interpretation.->command injection in emergency override audit logging|command injection|cwe-78|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|622|632|admin::adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|#ifdef _win32 const char* temp_dir = std::getenv("temp"); std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : ".") + "\\supercalc_emergency.log"; std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; #else const char* temp_dir = std::getenv("tmpdir"); std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : "/tmp") + "/supercalc_emergency.log"; std::string command = "sh -c \"echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path + "\""; #endif system(command.c_str());|arbitrary command execution if the temp or tmpdir environment variables contain shell metacharacters. also, the audit_path is not properly escaped.|emergency override authentication triggering the append_override_audit_event function with a manipulated environment.|use secure file operations (e.g., std::ofstream) instead of system() calls. sanitize all paths and avoid shell interpretation.

buffer overflow in evaluate_expression (result_buffer_)|buffer overflow|cwe-125|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|891|893|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|char formatted_result[64]; snprintf(formatted_result, sizeof(formatted_result), "%.10g", result); sprintf(result_buffer_, "result: %s", formatted_result);|buffer overflow in result_buffer_ (512 bytes) if formatted_result is manipulated to be very long (though unlikely with %.10g). sprintf does not check bounds.|a result that, when formatted, causes the sprintf output to exceed 512 bytes (unlikely but possible with very large numbers or special cases).|use snprintf instead of sprintf to ensure bounds checking. snprintf(result_buffer_, sizeof(result_buffer_), "result: %s", formatted_result);->buffer overflow in evaluate_expression (result_buffer_)|buffer overflow|cwe-125|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|891|893|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|char formatted_result[64]; snprintf(formatted_result, sizeof(formatted_result), "%.10g", result); sprintf(result_buffer_, "result: %s", formatted_result);|buffer overflow in result_buffer_ (512 bytes) if formatted_result is manipulated to be very long (though unlikely with %.10g). sprintf does not check bounds.|a result that, when formatted, causes the sprintf output to exceed 512 bytes (unlikely but possible with very large numbers or special cases).|use snprintf instead of sprintf to ensure bounds checking. snprintf(result_buffer_, sizeof(result_buffer_), "result: %s", formatted_result);

denial of service via infinite loop in factorial function|denial of service|cwe-835|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|312|320|math_engine::functionregistry::functionregistry (fact function)|unary_functions_["fact"] = [](double x) -> double { if (x < 0) return nan; if (x == 0 || x == 1) return 1; long long result = 1; for (long long i = 2; i <= static_cast<long long>(x); i++) { result *= i; } return static_cast<double>(result);|for very large x (e.g., 1e100), the loop will run for an impractical number of iterations, causing a denial of service.|calling fact() with a very large number (e.g., fact(1e100)).|add a maximum input limit (e.g., x > 20) and return an error or infinity for inputs that are too large.->denial of service via infinite loop in factorial function|denial of service|cwe-835|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|312|320|math_engine::functionregistry::functionregistry (fact function)|unary_functions_["fact"] = [](double x) -> double { if (x < 0) return nan; if (x == 0 || x == 1) return 1; long long result = 1; for (long long i = 2; i <= static_cast<long long>(x); i++) { result *= i; } return static_cast<double>(result);|for very large x (e.g., 1e100), the loop will run for an impractical number of iterations, causing a denial of service.|calling fact() with a very large number (e.g., fact(1e100)).|add a maximum input limit (e.g., x > 20) and return an error or infinity for inputs that are too large.

race condition in memorypool::allocate|race condition|cwe-362|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|123|158|memory::memorypool::allocate|std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(pool_mutex_); for (auto& block : blocks_) { if (!block.in_use && block.size >= size) { ... if (should_split) { ... fragment.data = base + size + split_metadata_bytes; fragment.size = remaining; ... blocks_.emplace_back(std::move(fragment)); } ... return base;|while the mutex protects the blocks_ vector, the fragment's data pointer is derived from base (which is part of an existing block). if another thread modifies the block concurrently (unlikely due to the mutex), it could lead to memory corruption. additionally, the ref_count is managed with relaxed memory ordering, which may not be safe.|concurrent calls to allocate() and deallocate() with specific timing.|ensure all operations on block metadata (e.g., splitting) are atomic or properly synchronized. use stronger memory ordering for ref_count operations.->race condition in memorypool::allocate|race condition|cwe-362|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|123|158|memory::memorypool::allocate|std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(pool_mutex_); for (auto& block : blocks_) { if (!block.in_use && block.size >= size) { ... if (should_split) { ... fragment.data = base + size + split_metadata_bytes; fragment.size = remaining; ... blocks_.emplace_back(std::move(fragment)); } ... return base;|while the mutex protects the blocks_ vector, the fragment's data pointer is derived from base (which is part of an existing block). if another thread modifies the block concurrently (unlikely due to the mutex), it could lead to memory corruption. additionally, the ref_count is managed with relaxed memory ordering, which may not be safe.|concurrent calls to allocate() and deallocate() with specific timing.|ensure all operations on block metadata (e.g., splitting) are atomic or properly synchronized. use stronger memory ordering for ref_count operations.

memory leak in memorypool::deallocate|memory leak|cwe-401|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|160|170|memory::memorypool::deallocate|void deallocate(void* ptr) { std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(pool_mutex_); for (auto& block : blocks_) { if (block.data == ptr) { if (block.ref_count.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed) == 1) { block.in_use = false; } return; } }|if a block's ref_count is > 1 when deallocate is called, the block is marked as not in_use but not freed. the memory is leaked because there is no mechanism to free it later when ref_count reaches 0.|deallocating a block with ref_count > 1 (e.g., due to a copy or move operation).|free the block's memory when ref_count reaches 0. ensure proper reference counting logic and free the memory in deallocate if ref_count becomes 0.->memory leak in memorypool::deallocate|memory leak|cwe-401|medium|0.9|enhanced_calc.cpp|160|170|memory::memorypool::deallocate|void deallocate(void* ptr) { std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(pool_mutex_); for (auto& block : blocks_) { if (block.data == ptr) { if (block.ref_count.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed) == 1) { block.in_use = false; } return; } }|if a block's ref_count is > 1 when deallocate is called, the block is marked as not in_use but not freed. the memory is leaked because there is no mechanism to free it later when ref_count reaches 0.|deallocating a block with ref_count > 1 (e.g., due to a copy or move operation).|free the block's memory when ref_count reaches 0. ensure proper reference counting logic and free the memory in deallocate if ref_count becomes 0.

missing bounds check in safe_string_copy|improper input validation|cwe-20|low|0.8|enhanced_calc.cpp|200|206|string_utils::safe_string_copy|inline void safe_string_copy(char* dest, const char* src, size_t dest_size) { if (!dest || !src || dest_size == 0) return; size_t src_len = strlen(src); if (src_len > dest_size) src_len = dest_size; memcpy(dest, src, src_len); dest[src_len] = '\0'; }|if dest_size is 1, src_len will be set to 1, and memcpy will copy 1 byte (no room for null terminator). then dest[1] = '\0' writes out of bounds.|calling safe_string_copy with dest_size = 1 and a non-empty src.|ensure dest_size is at least 1 for the null terminator. change the condition to: if (src_len >= dest_size) src_len = dest_size - 1; if (dest_size == 0) return; dest[src_len] = '\0';->missing bounds check in safe_string_copy|improper input validation|cwe-20|low|0.8|enhanced_calc.cpp|200|206|string_utils::safe_string_copy|inline void safe_string_copy(char* dest, const char* src, size_t dest_size) { if (!dest || !src || dest_size == 0) return; size_t src_len = strlen(src); if (src_len > dest_size) src_len = dest_size; memcpy(dest, src, src_len); dest[src_len] = '\0'; }|if dest_size is 1, src_len will be set to 1, and memcpy will copy 1 byte (no room for null terminator). then dest[1] = '\0' writes out of bounds.|calling safe_string_copy with dest_size = 1 and a non-empty src.|ensure dest_size is at least 1 for the null terminator. change the condition to: if (src_len >= dest_size) src_len = dest_size - 1; if (dest_size == 0) return; dest[src_len] = '\0';

sandboxing disabled by default|security feature disabled|cwe-1144|informational|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|72|72|config::security::enable_sandboxing|constexpr bool enable_sandboxing = false;|the application is not running in a sandboxed environment by default, which could limit the impact of exploits (e.g., command injection).|n/a (configuration issue).|enable sandboxing by default (set to true) and ensure the application is properly sandboxed at runtime.->sandboxing disabled by default|security feature disabled|cwe-1144|informational|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|72|72|config::security::enable_sandboxing|constexpr bool enable_sandboxing = false;|the application is not running in a sandboxed environment by default, which could limit the impact of exploits (e.g., command injection).|n/a (configuration issue).|enable sandboxing by default (set to true) and ensure the application is properly sandboxed at runtime.

uncontrolled resource consumption in evaluate_expression|denial of service|cwe-400|medium|0.8|enhanced_calc.cpp|830|880|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|double evaluate_expression(const std::string& expression) { ... if (expression.find('+') != std::string::npos) { ... } else if (expression.find('-') != std::string::npos && expression[0] != '-') { ... } else if (expression.find('*') != std::string::npos) { ... } else if (expression.find('/') != std::string::npos) { ... } else if (expression.find("fact") != std::string::npos) { ... } else { ... }|the function uses a naive approach to parse expressions, which can lead to incorrect parsing (e.g., '1+2*3' would be parsed as (1+2)*3 instead of 1+(2*3)). additionally, it may not handle all edge cases, leading to crashes or undefined behavior for malformed input.|evaluating expressions with operator precedence issues or malformed syntax.|use the proper ast-based parser (expressionparser and expressionevaluator) consistently instead of the ad-hoc parsing logic.

use of unsafe system() calls|dangerous function usage|cwe-78|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|567|567|file_ops::configloader::validate_config|int result = system(command.c_str());|the system() function is inherently unsafe as it invokes a shell, which can interpret metacharacters in the command string. this can lead to command injection if any part of the command is user-controlled.|any call to validate_config with user-controlled validator_path or config_file settings.|replace system() with safer alternatives like execve or direct file operations. avoid shell interpretation entirely.
